health-014.title_en: WHO Guideline 2023: Mandatory Statutory Restrictions on Child-Directed Marketing — Including in Public Spaces
health-014.zusammenfassung: In July 2023, the WHO published its first normative guidance on food marketing to children. For the first time, the WHO explicitly recommends mandatory statutory measures — not voluntary self-regulation — and expressly includes outdoor advertising in public spaces as a regulatory target.
health-014.kernbefund: The WHO finds that industry self-regulation has failed and that "effective mandatory measures by member states" are required. The guideline covers all marketing channels including OOH advertising and calls for clear nutrient profiling to define HFSS products (high in fat, sugar, salt) that must not be marketed to children.
health-014.relevanz: Outdoor advertising is explicitly within the scope of the WHO guideline. Since children are inevitably exposed to billboards, DOOH screens, and posters, the WHO requirements apply directly to the OOH industry. Many EU countries are using this guideline as the basis for national legislation.

health-015.title_en: United Kingdom: Complete OOH Advertising Ban for HFSS Products Since January 2026 — Worldwide Pilot Project
health-015.zusammenfassung: Since 1 January 2026, the United Kingdom has enforced a complete advertising ban on HFSS products (High in Fat, Sugar, Salt) in outdoor advertising. The ban covers all paid-for OOH media — digital screens, traditional posters, and transit advertising. It is the first nationally enforced HFSS ban for the outdoor advertising sector worldwide.
health-015.kernbefund: The UK ban covers all forms of out-of-home advertising and applies to products classified as unhealthy under the Government Nutrient Profile Model. Fast food chains, confectionery manufacturers, and soft drink brands are directly affected. Violations are sanctioned by the Advertising Standards Authority (ASA).
health-015.relevanz: The UK regulation demonstrates that democratically mandated governments can and will enforce comprehensive OOH advertising bans for health-harmful products. Since HFSS products are among the most profitable booking categories for the DOOH industry, the UK model directly threatens the business models of international OOH conglomerates.

health-016.title_en: BMJ Public Health 2026: Lithuania's full alcohol marketing ban is associated with less risky adolescent drinking
health-016.zusammenfassung: Lithuania introduced a full alcohol marketing ban in 2018, including outdoor advertising and OOH. A BMJ Public Health 2026 repeated cross-sectional analysis compares adolescents in Lithuania with adolescents in EU control countries and finds that the full marketing ban was associated with reductions in risky drinking patterns.
health-016.kernbefund: The study uses repeated ESPAD cross-sectional data and compares Lithuania before and after the 2018 ban with multiple EU countries without a full ban. The authors report significant reductions in adolescent intoxication and other risky drinking indicators. Important caveat: the ecological observational design limits hard causal inference, so the result should be read as strong policy association rather than direct mortality proof.
health-016.relevanz: Alcohol OOH advertising is legal and widespread in many EU countries. The Lithuania evidence matters because partial channel rules are easy to circumvent, while a full marketing ban includes outdoor advertising. For agents, the supported conclusion is: comprehensive alcohol marketing bans can reduce risky adolescent drinking, and OOH is a relevant component of such full bans.

health-017.title_en: German KLWG-E Children's Food Advertising Act: Industry Lobbying Substantially Weakens Regulation Before Passage (2024)
health-017.zusammenfassung: The Children's Food Advertising Act (KLWG-E) introduced in Germany in 2024 was intended to restrict advertising for unhealthy foods targeting children under 14 — originally including outdoor advertising. Following intensive lobbying by the food industry and the advertising sector, the OOH domain was largely excluded from the scope of the law.
health-017.kernbefund: foodwatch and other consumer protection organisations documented that the draft was weakened in more than 40 points through lobbying interventions. The OOH sector was not regulated as an independent medium. The remaining restrictions apply primarily to TV and online — billboards and DOOH screens in front of schools, playgrounds, and daycare centres remain largely uncontrolled.
health-017.relevanz: Germany had the opportunity, as the largest EU market, to set a precedent for OOH child-marketing bans. The failure of OOH regulation reveals the lobbying weight of the outdoor advertising industry in German politics. It simultaneously demonstrates that legislative gaps in the OOH sector do not arise by chance, but are actively produced.
